14.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In REAL environments (not vacuum), air resistance is significant for light, flat, or large-surface-area objects. A camera falls FASTER than a wide-brimmed hat. A coin falls FASTER than a sheet of paper.
- Fall speed ranking (fastest to slowest): solid metal/glass/stone → electronics/tools/ceramics → books/wood/fruit → hats/paper cups/empty bottles → paper/feathers/thin fabric/plastic bags.
- When asked 'which hits the ground first', DEFAULT to considering air resistance UNLESS the question explicitly states vacuum/no air. If two objects have similar density and shape, answer 'nearly simultaneous but depends on conditions'.
- Water/liquid poured from a container follows a PARABOLIC (curved) trajectory, not a straight line. Side view shows a downward-curving arc. The curve is more pronounced at low flow rates or long distances.
- An object WILL ROLL on a tilted surface if its contact surface is CIRCULAR/CURVED: balls on any slope, bottles lying sideways, circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook.
- An object will NOT roll if its contact surface is FLAT: books, boxes, phones, cups standing upright. These will SLIDE only if the tilt exceeds the friction angle (typically >15° on rough surfaces, >5° on smooth).
- A cylindrical candle holder LYING ON ITS SIDE on a table will roll if the table has even slight tilt. It will roll until it hits an obstacle or falls off the edge.
- Elastic bounce: a soft cushion thrown up and landing on a sofa WILL BOUNCE due to the sofa's elasticity → it may bounce off the sofa onto the floor.
- Inertia principle: quickly pulling out the BOTTOM plate from a stack → upper plates momentarily stay in place (inertia) → then drop vertically onto the table surface → they usually REMAIN STACKED (not topple), similar to the tablecloth trick.
- DENSITY AND BUOYANCY: Whole lemon floats (skin has air pockets, overall density ≈ 0.9-1.0). But LEMON SLICES SINK (cutting exposes dense flesh, loses air pocket buoyancy, flesh density > 1.0).
- Floating objects: most wood, whole citrus fruits (lemon, orange), empty plastic bottles, ice, wax/candles, cork.
- Sinking objects: lemon/orange SLICES, grapes, fresh eggs, stones, metal, glass, ceramics, coins.
- OUTDOOR WIND is a primary force for lightweight objects. Leaves on a deck move to the pool primarily by WIND, not gravity (decks are usually horizontal). Consider wind before gravity for any outdoor scene with light objects.
- Wind pushes liquid surfaces: wind from direction X → liquid surface tilts toward the opposite direction → may spill from the downwind side of the container.
- Ball rolling and hitting an obstacle → BOUNCES/DEFLECTS away from the obstacle, does not simply stop next to it. The bounce direction depends on the angle of impact.
- Domino/chain reaction: one falling object may strike adjacent objects → assess whether neighboring objects are within reach and whether the falling object has enough momentum.
- A phone standing upright on its edge will eventually fall over. The primary cause is that its equilibrium is UNSTABLE (very narrow base), and any tiny perturbation (vibration, air current) will topple it. The proximate cause of perturbation may be air movement, surface vibration, etc., not 'gravity' (gravity is always present; the question is what TRIGGERS the fall).
- On an inclined book surface: heavy objects with flat bottoms (like oranges sitting in a slight depression, or nuts with irregular shapes that wedge in place) may NOT move at small angles due to friction and surface irregularity. Don't assume everything slides/rolls on any tilt.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Spatial Containment) ---
- A container MUST be hollow or have an opening large enough for the object to pass through.
- Always check the OPENING DIAMETER of the container against the MAXIMUM CROSS-SECTION of the object, not just the main body.
- Protruding parts (handles, spouts, knobs, antennas, decorations) INCREASE the effective bounding box of an object. A teapot with handle can be 50-70% wider than its body alone.
- If the object's height EXCEEDS the container's internal depth, it can only be PARTIALLY inserted (sticks out above the rim), not fully contained.
- Check whether the container already holds other items inside. Existing items REDUCE available internal volume and may need to be removed first.
- Rigid objects (metal, glass, ceramic) must strictly satisfy dimensional constraints. Flexible objects (cloth, paper, rope) can bend or fold to fit smaller spaces.
- Consider BOTH orientations: an object that is too long horizontally may fit vertically, and vice versa. Always check the best-fit orientation.
- For building/doorway passage: width AND height AND ground-level obstacles (steps, curbs, thresholds) must ALL be checked. A vehicle may fit width-wise but be blocked by stairs.
- Public art installations of everyday objects (giant dustpan, giant chair) are 10-100x larger than real counterparts. Use nearby humans (~170cm tall) as size reference.
- Common container internal dimensions: standard mug opening ~8-9cm, cereal bowl ~14-16cm, shoebox ~30×18×11cm, standard doorway ~80-90cm wide × 200-210cm tall.
- Common object maximum cross-sections: apple ~7-9cm, baby head width ~11-15cm (newborn 11cm, 6-month 13-15cm), teapot with handle ~20cm, bicycle handlebar ~45-65cm.
- When the answer involves 'fits but tightly' vs 'fits with plenty of room': if the size margin is <20% of the container dimension, it is TIGHT; if >20%, there is ROOM TO SPARE.
- A bowl inverted over a cup as a lid: compare the BOWL'S RIM DIAMETER with the CUP'S RIM DIAMETER. If bowl rim < cup rim, the bowl falls inside rather than sitting on top.
- For 'can X pass through Y' questions: the object's MINIMUM cross-section along the direction of travel determines passability, not its volume.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shape Projection) ---
- Projection = the OUTLINE/SILHOUETTE seen along a specific viewing direction. It LOSES one dimension entirely and keeps the maximum extent in the other two dimensions.
- Cylinder: top view = CIRCLE, side view = RECTANGLE. This applies to cups, cans, candles, cylindrical lamp shades, pillars.
- Sphere: ALL views = CIRCLE. But an ELLIPSOID produces a CIRCLE only along its axis of symmetry; other views produce ELLIPSES.
- Cone: top view = CIRCLE (with center dot for apex), side view = TRIANGLE.
- Shallow tray/plate: top view = CIRCLE or RECTANGLE (its face shape), side view = a very thin LINE (nearly invisible, NOT a circle).
- A tennis/badminton racket head is an ELLIPSE, not a perfect circle. Its shadow shows an ELLIPTICAL outline with internal grid/string pattern.
- ATTACHMENTS NEVER DISAPPEAR in projection. Handles, spouts, knobs, bells, crowns, antennas ALL appear as additional shapes protruding from the main silhouette.
- Classic alarm clock top view = main body (circle or rectangle) + TWO SMALL CIRCLES on top (bells) + thin line (hammer). Not just a simple circle.
- Teapot/kettle side view = body silhouette + S-CURVED spout + C-CURVED handle + lid knob on top. Not just an oval.
- HOLLOW/MESH/FRAMEWORK structures cast LINE-PATTERN shadows, not solid shadows. Woven chair → striped shadow. Racket strings → grid shadow. Fence → parallel line shadow. Bicycle wheel spokes → radial line shadow.
- Chair side profile = inverted 'r' shape: vertical line (backrest) → horizontal line (seat) → vertical line down (front leg), with rear leg going diagonally from seat back to floor.
- Person riding a bicycle, viewed from DIRECTLY ABOVE = T-shape (body + handlebars form the horizontal bar, bicycle frame forms the vertical bar).
- When multiple spheres/rounded shapes are FUSED together (like Mickey Mouse shape = 1 large + 2 small spheres), the top-view outline is a CONTINUOUS ROUNDED-TRIANGLE envelope, NOT three separate circles.
- A bowl's top view is a CIRCLE if the bowl is perfectly round, but an ELLIPSE if (a) the bowl itself is oval-shaped, or (b) the viewing angle is not perfectly vertical.
- Knife top view = IRREGULAR shape: blade has a curved edge on one side and straight edge on the other, tapering to a point, plus a differently-shaped handle. It is NOT a simple rectangle.
- Glass jewelry box top view = RECTANGLE in most cases (length ≠ width), not a perfect square. Most jewelry boxes are elongated.
- Round lounge chair/daybed top view = OVAL/ELLIPTICAL ring (not a perfect circle), because the head-end and foot-end differ in width.
- Ancient vessel with lid, top view = CONCENTRIC CIRCLES: outer circle = vessel rim, inner circle = lid. If it has handles, they protrude OUTSIDE the outer circle.
- For SHADOW questions specifically: (1) Overhead noon sun → shadow ≈ top-view projection, (2) Angled sun → shadow is stretched/distorted, (3) Framework objects cast line shadows not solid shadows, (4) Transparent/translucent parts cast lighter or no shadow.
- Multiple clothespins stacked together: side profile shows CURVED outlines (each clothespin has curved arms), not straight lines.
- Hedge trimmer/pruning shears top view: complex irregular shape with handles + blade mechanism, hard to determine precisely → if uncertain, 'cannot determine' may be valid for unusual tools.

- Floating objects: most wood, whole citrus fruits (lemon, orange), empty plastic bottles, ice, wax/candles, cork.
- Sinking objects: lemon/orange SLICES, grapes, fresh eggs, stones, metal, glass, ceramics, coins.
- OUTDOOR WIND is a primary force for lightweight objects. Leaves on a deck move to the pool primarily by WIND, not gravity (decks are usually horizontal). Consider wind before gravity for any outdoor scene with light objects.
- Wind pushes liquid surfaces: wind from direction X → liquid surface tilts toward the opposite direction → may spill from the downwind side of the container.
- Ball rolling and hitting an obstacle → BOUNCES/DEFLECTS away from the obstacle, does not simply stop next to it. The bounce direction depends on the angle of impact.
- Domino/chain reaction: one falling object may strike adjacent objects → assess whether neighboring objects are within reach and whether the falling object has enough momentum.
- A phone standing upright on its edge will eventually fall over. The primary cause is that its equilibrium is UNSTABLE (very narrow base), and any tiny perturbation (vibration, air current) will topple it. The proximate cause of perturbation may be air movement, surface vibration, etc., not 'gravity' (gravity is always present; the question is what TRIGGERS the fall).
- On an inclined book surface: heavy objects with flat bottoms (like oranges sitting in a slight depression, or nuts with irregular shapes that wedge in place) may NOT move at small angles due to friction and surface irregularity. Don't assume everything slides/rolls on any tilt.

- [Parallelism]: Both 'chandelier' and 'wall' present a 'frontal'/'frontal' view. Their front planes are PARALLEL.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shape Projection) ---
- Projection = the OUTLINE/SILHOUETTE seen along a specific viewing direction. It LOSES one dimension entirely and keeps the maximum extent in the other two dimensions.
- Cylinder: top view = CIRCLE, side view = RECTANGLE. This applies to cups, cans, candles, cylindrical lamp shades, pillars.
- Sphere: ALL views = CIRCLE. But an ELLIPSOID produces a CIRCLE only along its axis of symmetry; other views produce ELLIPSES.
- Cone: top view = CIRCLE (with center dot for apex), side view = TRIANGLE.
- Shallow tray/plate: top view = CIRCLE or RECTANGLE (its face shape), side view = a very thin LINE (nearly invisible, NOT a circle).
- A tennis/badminton racket head is an ELLIPSE, not a perfect circle. Its shadow shows an ELLIPTICAL outline with internal grid/string pattern.
- ATTACHMENTS NEVER DISAPPEAR in projection. Handles, spouts, knobs, bells, crowns, antennas ALL appear as additional shapes protruding from the main silhouette.
- Classic alarm clock top view = main body (circle or rectangle) + TWO SMALL CIRCLES on top (bells) + thin line (hammer). Not just a simple circle.
- Teapot/kettle side view = body silhouette + S-CURVED spout + C-CURVED handle + lid knob on top. Not just an oval.
- HOLLOW/MESH/FRAMEWORK structures cast LINE-PATTERN shadows, not solid shadows. Woven chair → striped shadow. Racket strings → grid shadow. Fence → parallel line shadow. Bicycle wheel spokes → radial line shadow.
- Chair side profile = inverted 'r' shape: vertical line (backrest) → horizontal line (seat) → vertical line down (front leg), with rear leg going diagonally from seat back to floor.
- Person riding a bicycle, viewed from DIRECTLY ABOVE = T-shape (body + handlebars form the horizontal bar, bicycle frame forms the vertical bar).
- When multiple spheres/rounded shapes are FUSED together (like Mickey Mouse shape = 1 large + 2 small spheres), the top-view outline is a CONTINUOUS ROUNDED-TRIANGLE envelope, NOT three separate circles.
- A bowl's top view is a CIRCLE if the bowl is perfectly round, but an ELLIPSE if (a) the bowl itself is oval-shaped, or (b) the viewing angle is not perfectly vertical.
- Knife top view = IRREGULAR shape: blade has a curved edge on one side and straight edge on the other, tapering to a point, plus a differently-shaped handle. It is NOT a simple rectangle.
- Glass jewelry box top view = RECTANGLE in most cases (length ≠ width), not a perfect square. Most jewelry boxes are elongated.
- Round lounge chair/daybed top view = OVAL/ELLIPTICAL ring (not a perfect circle), because the head-end and foot-end differ in width.
- Ancient vessel with lid, top view = CONCENTRIC CIRCLES: outer circle = vessel rim, inner circle = lid. If it has handles, they protrude OUTSIDE the outer circle.
- For SHADOW questions specifically: (1) Overhead noon sun → shadow ≈ top-view projection, (2) Angled sun → shadow is stretched/distorted, (3) Framework objects cast line shadows not solid shadows, (4) Transparent/translucent parts cast lighter or no shadow.
- Multiple clothespins stacked together: side profile shows CURVED outlines (each clothespin has curved arms), not straight lines.
- Hedge trimmer/pruning shears top view: complex irregular shape with handles + blade mechanism, hard to determine precisely → if uncertain, 'cannot determine' may be valid for unusual tools.

=== Single Object Geometric States ===
- [tall pot in the background with the wide mouth]: Presents a 'frontal' view. It is aligned with the camera axis.
- [small jug from the center of the photo]: Presents a 'frontal' view. It is aligned with the camera axis.

=== Absolute Spatial Relationships (Camera Perspective) ===
- [Depth]: small jug from the center of the photo is CLOSER to the camera than tall pot in the background with the wide mouth.
- [Horizontal]: tall pot in the background with the wide mouth is to the LEFT of small jug from the center of the photo.

=== Pairwise Geometric & Orientation Deductions ===
- [Parallelism]: Both 'tall pot in the background with the wide mouth' and 'small jug from the center of the photo' present a 'frontal'/'frontal' view. Their front planes are PARALLEL.
- [Relative Direction]: Both point 'camera'. They are facing the SAME direction.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Spatial Containment) ---
- A container MUST be hollow or have an opening large enough for the object to pass through.
- Always check the OPENING DIAMETER of the container against the MAXIMUM CROSS-SECTION of the object, not just the main body.
- Protruding parts (handles, spouts, knobs, antennas, decorations) INCREASE the effective bounding box of an object. A teapot with handle can be 50-70% wider than its body alone.
- If the object's height EXCEEDS the container's internal depth, it can only be PARTIALLY inserted (sticks out above the rim), not fully contained.
- Check whether the container already holds other items inside. Existing items REDUCE available internal volume and may need to be removed first.
- Rigid objects (metal, glass, ceramic) must strictly satisfy dimensional constraints. Flexible objects (cloth, paper, rope) can bend or fold to fit smaller spaces.
- Consider BOTH orientations: an object that is too long horizontally may fit vertically, and vice versa. Always check the best-fit orientation.
- For building/doorway passage: width AND height AND ground-level obstacles (steps, curbs, thresholds) must ALL be checked. A vehicle may fit width-wise but be blocked by stairs.
- Public art installations of everyday objects (giant dustpan, giant chair) are 10-100x larger than real counterparts. Use nearby humans (~170cm tall) as size reference.
- Common container internal dimensions: standard mug opening ~8-9cm, cereal bowl ~14-16cm, shoebox ~30×18×11cm, standard doorway ~80-90cm wide × 200-210cm tall.
- Common object maximum cross-sections: apple ~7-9cm, baby head width ~11-15cm (newborn 11cm, 6-month 13-15cm), teapot with handle ~20cm, bicycle handlebar ~45-65cm.
- When the answer involves 'fits but tightly' vs 'fits with plenty of room': if the size margin is <20% of the container dimension, it is TIGHT; if >20%, there is ROOM TO SPARE.
- A bowl inverted over a cup as a lid: compare the BOWL'S RIM DIAMETER with the CUP'S RIM DIAMETER. If bowl rim < cup rim, the bowl falls inside rather than sitting on top.
- For 'can X pass through Y' questions: the object's MINIMUM cross-section along the direction of travel determines passability, not its volume.

100.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In REAL environments (not vacuum), air resistance is significant for light, flat, or large-surface-area objects. A camera falls FASTER than a wide-brimmed hat. A coin falls FASTER than a sheet of paper.
- Fall speed ranking (fastest to slowest): solid metal/glass/stone → electronics/tools/ceramics → books/wood/fruit → hats/paper cups/empty bottles → paper/feathers/thin fabric/plastic bags.
- When asked 'which hits the ground first', DEFAULT to considering air resistance UNLESS the question explicitly states vacuum/no air. If two objects have similar density and shape, answer 'nearly simultaneous but depends on conditions'.
- Water/liquid poured from a container follows a PARABOLIC (curved) trajectory, not a straight line. Side view shows a downward-curving arc. The curve is more pronounced at low flow rates or long distances.
- An object WILL ROLL on a tilted surface if its contact surface is CIRCULAR/CURVED: balls on any slope, bottles lying sideways, circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook.
- An object will NOT roll if its contact surface is FLAT: books, boxes, phones, cups standing upright. These will SLIDE only if the tilt exceeds the friction angle (typically >15° on rough surfaces, >5° on smooth).
- A cylindrical candle holder LYING ON ITS SIDE on a table will roll if the table has even slight tilt. It will roll until it hits an obstacle or falls off the edge.
- Elastic bounce: a soft cushion thrown up and landing on a sofa WILL BOUNCE due to the sofa's elasticity → it may bounce off the sofa onto the floor.
- Inertia principle: quickly pulling out the BOTTOM plate from a stack → upper plates momentarily stay in place (inertia) → then drop vertically onto the table surface → they usually REMAIN STACKED (not topple), similar to the tablecloth trick.
- DENSITY AND BUOYANCY: Whole lemon floats (skin has air pockets, overall density ≈ 0.9-1.0). But LEMON SLICES SINK (cutting exposes dense flesh, loses air pocket buoyancy, flesh density > 1.0).
- Floating objects: most wood, whole citrus fruits (lemon, orange), empty plastic bottles, ice, wax/candles, cork.
- Sinking objects: lemon/orange SLICES, grapes, fresh eggs, stones, metal, glass, ceramics, coins.
- OUTDOOR WIND is a primary force for lightweight objects. Leaves on a deck move to the pool primarily by WIND, not gravity (decks are usually horizontal). Consider wind before gravity for any outdoor scene with light objects.
- Wind pushes liquid surfaces: wind from direction X → liquid surface tilts toward the opposite direction → may spill from the downwind side of the container.
- Ball rolling and hitting an obstacle → BOUNCES/DEFLECTS away from the obstacle, does not simply stop next to it. The bounce direction depends on the angle of impact.
- Domino/chain reaction: one falling object may strike adjacent objects → assess whether neighboring objects are within reach and whether the falling object has enough momentum.
- A phone standing upright on its edge will eventually fall over. The primary cause is that its equilibrium is UNSTABLE (very narrow base), and any tiny perturbation (vibration, air current) will topple it. The proximate cause of perturbation may be air movement, surface vibration, etc., not 'gravity' (gravity is always present; the question is what TRIGGERS the fall).
- On an inclined book surface: heavy objects with flat bottoms (like oranges sitting in a slight depression, or nuts with irregular shapes that wedge in place) may NOT move at small angles due to friction and surface irregularity. Don't assume everything slides/rolls on any tilt.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Spatial Containment) ---
- A container MUST be hollow or have an opening large enough for the object to pass through.
- Always check the OPENING DIAMETER of the container against the MAXIMUM CROSS-SECTION of the object, not just the main body.
- Protruding parts (handles, spouts, knobs, antennas, decorations) INCREASE the effective bounding box of an object. A teapot with handle can be 50-70% wider than its body alone.
- If the object's height EXCEEDS the container's internal depth, it can only be PARTIALLY inserted (sticks out above the rim), not fully contained.
- Check whether the container already holds other items inside. Existing items REDUCE available internal volume and may need to be removed first.
- Rigid objects (metal, glass, ceramic) must strictly satisfy dimensional constraints. Flexible objects (cloth, paper, rope) can bend or fold to fit smaller spaces.
- Consider BOTH orientations: an object that is too long horizontally may fit vertically, and vice versa. Always check the best-fit orientation.
- For building/doorway passage: width AND height AND ground-level obstacles (steps, curbs, thresholds) must ALL be checked. A vehicle may fit width-wise but be blocked by stairs.
- Public art installations of everyday objects (giant dustpan, giant chair) are 10-100x larger than real counterparts. Use nearby humans (~170cm tall) as size reference.
- Common container internal dimensions: standard mug opening ~8-9cm, cereal bowl ~14-16cm, shoebox ~30×18×11cm, standard doorway ~80-90cm wide × 200-210cm tall.
- Common object maximum cross-sections: apple ~7-9cm, baby head width ~11-15cm (newborn 11cm, 6-month 13-15cm), teapot with handle ~20cm, bicycle handlebar ~45-65cm.
- When the answer involves 'fits but tightly' vs 'fits with plenty of room': if the size margin is <20% of the container dimension, it is TIGHT; if >20%, there is ROOM TO SPARE.
- A bowl inverted over a cup as a lid: compare the BOWL'S RIM DIAMETER with the CUP'S RIM DIAMETER. If bowl rim < cup rim, the bowl falls inside rather than sitting on top.
- For 'can X pass through Y' questions: the object's MINIMUM cross-section along the direction of travel determines passability, not its volume.

139.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In REAL environments (not vacuum), air resistance is significant for light, flat, or large-surface-area objects. A camera falls FASTER than a wide-brimmed hat. A coin falls FASTER than a sheet of paper.
- Fall speed ranking (fastest to slowest): solid metal/glass/stone → electronics/tools/ceramics → books/wood/fruit → hats/paper cups/empty bottles → paper/feathers/thin fabric/plastic bags.
- When asked 'which hits the ground first', DEFAULT to considering air resistance UNLESS the question explicitly states vacuum/no air. If two objects have similar density and shape, answer 'nearly simultaneous but depends on conditions'.
- Water/liquid poured from a container follows a PARABOLIC (curved) trajectory, not a straight line. Side view shows a downward-curving arc. The curve is more pronounced at low flow rates or long distances.
- An object WILL ROLL on a tilted surface if its contact surface is CIRCULAR/CURVED: balls on any slope, bottles lying sideways, circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook.
- An object will NOT roll if its contact surface is FLAT: books, boxes, phones, cups standing upright. These will SLIDE only if the tilt exceeds the friction angle (typically >15° on rough surfaces, >5° on smooth).
- A cylindrical candle holder LYING ON ITS SIDE on a table will roll if the table has even slight tilt. It will roll until it hits an obstacle or falls off the edge.
- Elastic bounce: a soft cushion thrown up and landing on a sofa WILL BOUNCE due to the sofa's elasticity → it may bounce off the sofa onto the floor.
- Inertia principle: quickly pulling out the BOTTOM plate from a stack → upper plates momentarily stay in place (inertia) → then drop vertically onto the table surface → they usually REMAIN STACKED (not topple), similar to the tablecloth trick.
- DENSITY AND BUOYANCY: Whole lemon floats (skin has air pockets, overall density ≈ 0.9-1.0). But LEMON SLICES SINK (cutting exposes dense flesh, loses air pocket buoyancy, flesh density > 1.0).
- Floating objects: most wood, whole citrus fruits (lemon, orange), empty plastic bottles, ice, wax/candles, cork.
- Sinking objects: lemon/orange SLICES, grapes, fresh eggs, stones, metal, glass, ceramics, coins.
- OUTDOOR WIND is a primary force for lightweight objects. Leaves on a deck move to the pool primarily by WIND, not gravity (decks are usually horizontal). Consider wind before gravity for any outdoor scene with light objects.
- Wind pushes liquid surfaces: wind from direction X → liquid surface tilts toward the opposite direction → may spill from the downwind side of the container.
- Ball rolling and hitting an obstacle → BOUNCES/DEFLECTS away from the obstacle, does not simply stop next to it. The bounce direction depends on the angle of impact.
- Domino/chain reaction: one falling object may strike adjacent objects → assess whether neighboring objects are within reach and whether the falling object has enough momentum.
- A phone standing upright on its edge will eventually fall over. The primary cause is that its equilibrium is UNSTABLE (very narrow base), and any tiny perturbation (vibration, air current) will topple it. The proximate cause of perturbation may be air movement, surface vibration, etc., not 'gravity' (gravity is always present; the question is what TRIGGERS the fall).
- On an inclined book surface: heavy objects with flat bottoms (like oranges sitting in a slight depression, or nuts with irregular shapes that wedge in place) may NOT move at small angles due to friction and surface irregularity. Don't assume everything slides/rolls on any tilt.

142.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- FUNDAMENTAL RULE: An object is stable if and only if the vertical projection of its center of gravity falls WITHIN its base of support (the contact area with the supporting surface).
- Wider base → more stable. Lower center of gravity → more stable. The most UNSTABLE object in a group is the one with the SMALLEST base of support relative to its height.
- A wooden spoon standing on its narrow handle edge is LESS stable than dried flowers in a bunch (flowers have a wider spread base). Narrow edge/line contact = extremely unstable.
- MASS RATIO RULE for perturbation: If perturbing_mass / structure_mass < 1:100, the perturbation has NEGLIGIBLE effect on normal structures. A bird (0.1-5kg) landing on a lamp post (50-200kg fixed to ground) causes NO noticeable effect.
- EXCEPTION to mass ratio rule: PRECISION-BALANCED structures (stone balancing, card houses, objects balanced on a point/edge) can be toppled by even 1:10000 mass ratios. A single water drop (~0.05g) CAN collapse a balanced stone structure (~5kg) by creating a tiny off-center moment.
- A water drop on a precision stone balance → structure WILL COLLAPSE. Categorize: is this a normal stable placement or a precision balance? Precision balances are inherently at the tipping point.
- HUMANS have ACTIVE balance recovery ability, unlike passive objects. An adult can recover from moderate pushes. A child has weaker recovery. On slippery/steep surfaces, recovery is harder. If center of gravity is already far outside the base (e.g., leaning far over a ladder edge), even adults may fail to recover.
- Decision tree for 'will person fall': (1) How far is COG from base edge? (2) Surface friction (wet/dry/steep)? (3) Can they grab something? (4) Adult or child? → Child on steep surface with no handhold and shifted COG = likely falls. Adult on flat surface with slight push = recovers.
- Removing an object from a stack: removing the TOP item makes the remaining stack MORE stable (lower COG). Removing the BOTTOM item causes upper items to DROP but often MAINTAIN their relative arrangement. Removing a MIDDLE item depends on whether upper items can bridge the gap.
- If each object in an arrangement has its OWN independent support on the table surface, removing one object does NOT affect others. Example: pencil resting on top of a walnut on a table → remove pencil → walnut stays (it has its own stable base on the table).
- Round objects on ANY tilted surface will roll. Flat-bottomed objects on slightly tilted surfaces usually stay put. A circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook → WILL ROLL OFF. A rectangular eraser on the same notebook → likely stays.
- Critical tilt angles: rough surface ~15° before sliding starts, smooth surface ~5-8°, round objects may roll at even 1-2° tilt.
- If an object appears to stand upright without visible support but physics says it shouldn't (e.g., a floppy bag standing upright), it is most likely LEANING AGAINST an unseen object outside the frame. Do not attribute it to the object's own shape.
- Pushing furniture: force applied ABOVE the center of gravity tends to cause TIPPING/TOPPLING. Force applied BELOW the center of gravity tends to cause SLIDING. Pushing the top of a chair backrest → the chair will likely TIP OVER, not slide.
- Sitting on the edge of a bench: if the person's weight is within the bench's base of support and the bench is heavy/stable, NOTHING happens. A light blanket draped over the bench does not slide off just because someone sits on one end (unless they directly push it).
- A child leaning outward on a ladder: the child's weight shifts the combined COG outside the ladder's base → the ladder WILL TIP. The child is a significant fraction of the ladder's weight (mass ratio 20kg child : 5kg ladder = 4:1 → very significant).
- Normal force explanation: a towel on a table doesn't fall through because the table exerts an upward NORMAL FORCE (Newton's 3rd law). However, if the question asks WHY the towel stays specifically at its position (not sliding off), the answer is that the towel's COG projection is within the table surface (it is in a STABLE EQUILIBRIUM position).

160.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In REAL environments (not vacuum), air resistance is significant for light, flat, or large-surface-area objects. A camera falls FASTER than a wide-brimmed hat. A coin falls FASTER than a sheet of paper.
- Fall speed ranking (fastest to slowest): solid metal/glass/stone → electronics/tools/ceramics → books/wood/fruit → hats/paper cups/empty bottles → paper/feathers/thin fabric/plastic bags.
- When asked 'which hits the ground first', DEFAULT to considering air resistance UNLESS the question explicitly states vacuum/no air. If two objects have similar density and shape, answer 'nearly simultaneous but depends on conditions'.
- Water/liquid poured from a container follows a PARABOLIC (curved) trajectory, not a straight line. Side view shows a downward-curving arc. The curve is more pronounced at low flow rates or long distances.
- An object WILL ROLL on a tilted surface if its contact surface is CIRCULAR/CURVED: balls on any slope, bottles lying sideways, circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook.
- An object will NOT roll if its contact surface is FLAT: books, boxes, phones, cups standing upright. These will SLIDE only if the tilt exceeds the friction angle (typically >15° on rough surfaces, >5° on smooth).
- A cylindrical candle holder LYING ON ITS SIDE on a table will roll if the table has even slight tilt. It will roll until it hits an obstacle or falls off the edge.
- Elastic bounce: a soft cushion thrown up and landing on a sofa WILL BOUNCE due to the sofa's elasticity → it may bounce off the sofa onto the floor.
- Inertia principle: quickly pulling out the BOTTOM plate from a stack → upper plates momentarily stay in place (inertia) → then drop vertically onto the table surface → they usually REMAIN STACKED (not topple), similar to the tablecloth trick.
- DENSITY AND BUOYANCY: Whole lemon floats (skin has air pockets, overall density ≈ 0.9-1.0). But LEMON SLICES SINK (cutting exposes dense flesh, loses air pocket buoyancy, flesh density > 1.0).
- Floating objects: most wood, whole citrus fruits (lemon, orange), empty plastic bottles, ice, wax/candles, cork.
- Sinking objects: lemon/orange SLICES, grapes, fresh eggs, stones, metal, glass, ceramics, coins.
- OUTDOOR WIND is a primary force for lightweight objects. Leaves on a deck move to the pool primarily by WIND, not gravity (decks are usually horizontal). Consider wind before gravity for any outdoor scene with light objects.
- Wind pushes liquid surfaces: wind from direction X → liquid surface tilts toward the opposite direction → may spill from the downwind side of the container.
- Ball rolling and hitting an obstacle → BOUNCES/DEFLECTS away from the obstacle, does not simply stop next to it. The bounce direction depends on the angle of impact.
- Domino/chain reaction: one falling object may strike adjacent objects → assess whether neighboring objects are within reach and whether the falling object has enough momentum.
- A phone standing upright on its edge will eventually fall over. The primary cause is that its equilibrium is UNSTABLE (very narrow base), and any tiny perturbation (vibration, air current) will topple it. The proximate cause of perturbation may be air movement, surface vibration, etc., not 'gravity' (gravity is always present; the question is what TRIGGERS the fall).
- On an inclined book surface: heavy objects with flat bottoms (like oranges sitting in a slight depression, or nuts with irregular shapes that wedge in place) may NOT move at small angles due to friction and surface irregularity. Don't assume everything slides/rolls on any tilt.

162.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- FUNDAMENTAL RULE: An object is stable if and only if the vertical projection of its center of gravity falls WITHIN its base of support (the contact area with the supporting surface).
- Wider base → more stable. Lower center of gravity → more stable. The most UNSTABLE object in a group is the one with the SMALLEST base of support relative to its height.
- A wooden spoon standing on its narrow handle edge is LESS stable than dried flowers in a bunch (flowers have a wider spread base). Narrow edge/line contact = extremely unstable.
- MASS RATIO RULE for perturbation: If perturbing_mass / structure_mass < 1:100, the perturbation has NEGLIGIBLE effect on normal structures. A bird (0.1-5kg) landing on a lamp post (50-200kg fixed to ground) causes NO noticeable effect.
- EXCEPTION to mass ratio rule: PRECISION-BALANCED structures (stone balancing, card houses, objects balanced on a point/edge) can be toppled by even 1:10000 mass ratios. A single water drop (~0.05g) CAN collapse a balanced stone structure (~5kg) by creating a tiny off-center moment.
- A water drop on a precision stone balance → structure WILL COLLAPSE. Categorize: is this a normal stable placement or a precision balance? Precision balances are inherently at the tipping point.
- HUMANS have ACTIVE balance recovery ability, unlike passive objects. An adult can recover from moderate pushes. A child has weaker recovery. On slippery/steep surfaces, recovery is harder. If center of gravity is already far outside the base (e.g., leaning far over a ladder edge), even adults may fail to recover.
- Decision tree for 'will person fall': (1) How far is COG from base edge? (2) Surface friction (wet/dry/steep)? (3) Can they grab something? (4) Adult or child? → Child on steep surface with no handhold and shifted COG = likely falls. Adult on flat surface with slight push = recovers.
- Removing an object from a stack: removing the TOP item makes the remaining stack MORE stable (lower COG). Removing the BOTTOM item causes upper items to DROP but often MAINTAIN their relative arrangement. Removing a MIDDLE item depends on whether upper items can bridge the gap.
- If each object in an arrangement has its OWN independent support on the table surface, removing one object does NOT affect others. Example: pencil resting on top of a walnut on a table → remove pencil → walnut stays (it has its own stable base on the table).
- Round objects on ANY tilted surface will roll. Flat-bottomed objects on slightly tilted surfaces usually stay put. A circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook → WILL ROLL OFF. A rectangular eraser on the same notebook → likely stays.
- Critical tilt angles: rough surface ~15° before sliding starts, smooth surface ~5-8°, round objects may roll at even 1-2° tilt.
- If an object appears to stand upright without visible support but physics says it shouldn't (e.g., a floppy bag standing upright), it is most likely LEANING AGAINST an unseen object outside the frame. Do not attribute it to the object's own shape.
- Pushing furniture: force applied ABOVE the center of gravity tends to cause TIPPING/TOPPLING. Force applied BELOW the center of gravity tends to cause SLIDING. Pushing the top of a chair backrest → the chair will likely TIP OVER, not slide.
- Sitting on the edge of a bench: if the person's weight is within the bench's base of support and the bench is heavy/stable, NOTHING happens. A light blanket draped over the bench does not slide off just because someone sits on one end (unless they directly push it).
- A child leaning outward on a ladder: the child's weight shifts the combined COG outside the ladder's base → the ladder WILL TIP. The child is a significant fraction of the ladder's weight (mass ratio 20kg child : 5kg ladder = 4:1 → very significant).
- Normal force explanation: a towel on a table doesn't fall through because the table exerts an upward NORMAL FORCE (Newton's 3rd law). However, if the question asks WHY the towel stays specifically at its position (not sliding off), the answer is that the towel's COG projection is within the table surface (it is in a STABLE EQUILIBRIUM position).

--- Domain Rules (Spatial Containment) ---
- A container MUST be hollow or have an opening large enough for the object to pass through.

- When the answer involves 'fits but tightly' vs 'fits with plenty of room': if the size margin is <20% of the container dimension, it is TIGHT; if >20%, there is ROOM TO SPARE.

201.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In REAL environments (not vacuum), air resistance is significant for light, flat, or large-surface-area objects. A camera falls FASTER than a wide-brimmed hat. A coin falls FASTER than a sheet of paper.
- Fall speed ranking (fastest to slowest): solid metal/glass/stone → electronics/tools/ceramics → books/wood/fruit → hats/paper cups/empty bottles → paper/feathers/thin fabric/plastic bags.
- When asked 'which hits the ground first', DEFAULT to considering air resistance UNLESS the question explicitly states vacuum/no air. If two objects have similar density and shape, answer 'nearly simultaneous but depends on conditions'.
- Water/liquid poured from a container follows a PARABOLIC (curved) trajectory, not a straight line. Side view shows a downward-curving arc. The curve is more pronounced at low flow rates or long distances.
- An object WILL ROLL on a tilted surface if its contact surface is CIRCULAR/CURVED: balls on any slope, bottles lying sideways, circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook.
- An object will NOT roll if its contact surface is FLAT: books, boxes, phones, cups standing upright. These will SLIDE only if the tilt exceeds the friction angle (typically >15° on rough surfaces, >5° on smooth).
- A cylindrical candle holder LYING ON ITS SIDE on a table will roll if the table has even slight tilt. It will roll until it hits an obstacle or falls off the edge.
- Elastic bounce: a soft cushion thrown up and landing on a sofa WILL BOUNCE due to the sofa's elasticity → it may bounce off the sofa onto the floor.
- Inertia principle: quickly pulling out the BOTTOM plate from a stack → upper plates momentarily stay in place (inertia) → then drop vertically onto the table surface → they usually REMAIN STACKED (not topple), similar to the tablecloth trick.
- DENSITY AND BUOYANCY: Whole lemon floats (skin has air pockets, overall density ≈ 0.9-1.0). But LEMON SLICES SINK (cutting exposes dense flesh, loses air pocket buoyancy, flesh density > 1.0).
- Floating objects: most wood, whole citrus fruits (lemon, orange), empty plastic bottles, ice, wax/candles, cork.
- Sinking objects: lemon/orange SLICES, grapes, fresh eggs, stones, metal, glass, ceramics, coins.
- OUTDOOR WIND is a primary force for lightweight objects. Leaves on a deck move to the pool primarily by WIND, not gravity (decks are usually horizontal). Consider wind before gravity for any outdoor scene with light objects.
- Wind pushes liquid surfaces: wind from direction X → liquid surface tilts toward the opposite direction → may spill from the downwind side of the container.
- Ball rolling and hitting an obstacle → BOUNCES/DEFLECTS away from the obstacle, does not simply stop next to it. The bounce direction depends on the angle of impact.
- Domino/chain reaction: one falling object may strike adjacent objects → assess whether neighboring objects are within reach and whether the falling object has enough momentum.
- A phone standing upright on its edge will eventually fall over. The primary cause is that its equilibrium is UNSTABLE (very narrow base), and any tiny perturbation (vibration, air current) will topple it. The proximate cause of perturbation may be air movement, surface vibration, etc., not 'gravity' (gravity is always present; the question is what TRIGGERS the fall).
- On an inclined book surface: heavy objects with flat bottoms (like oranges sitting in a slight depression, or nuts with irregular shapes that wedge in place) may NOT move at small angles due to friction and surface irregularity. Don't assume everything slides/rolls on any tilt.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shape Projection) ---
- Projection = the OUTLINE/SILHOUETTE seen along a specific viewing direction. It LOSES one dimension entirely and keeps the maximum extent in the other two dimensions.
- Cylinder: top view = CIRCLE, side view = RECTANGLE. This applies to cups, cans, candles, cylindrical lamp shades, pillars.
- Sphere: ALL views = CIRCLE. But an ELLIPSOID produces a CIRCLE only along its axis of symmetry; other views produce ELLIPSES.
- Cone: top view = CIRCLE (with center dot for apex), side view = TRIANGLE.
- Shallow tray/plate: top view = CIRCLE or RECTANGLE (its face shape), side view = a very thin LINE (nearly invisible, NOT a circle).
- A tennis/badminton racket head is an ELLIPSE, not a perfect circle. Its shadow shows an ELLIPTICAL outline with internal grid/string pattern.
- ATTACHMENTS NEVER DISAPPEAR in projection. Handles, spouts, knobs, bells, crowns, antennas ALL appear as additional shapes protruding from the main silhouette.
- Classic alarm clock top view = main body (circle or rectangle) + TWO SMALL CIRCLES on top (bells) + thin line (hammer). Not just a simple circle.
- Teapot/kettle side view = body silhouette + S-CURVED spout + C-CURVED handle + lid knob on top. Not just an oval.
- HOLLOW/MESH/FRAMEWORK structures cast LINE-PATTERN shadows, not solid shadows. Woven chair → striped shadow. Racket strings → grid shadow. Fence → parallel line shadow. Bicycle wheel spokes → radial line shadow.
- Chair side profile = inverted 'r' shape: vertical line (backrest) → horizontal line (seat) → vertical line down (front leg), with rear leg going diagonally from seat back to floor.
- Person riding a bicycle, viewed from DIRECTLY ABOVE = T-shape (body + handlebars form the horizontal bar, bicycle frame forms the vertical bar).
- When multiple spheres/rounded shapes are FUSED together (like Mickey Mouse shape = 1 large + 2 small spheres), the top-view outline is a CONTINUOUS ROUNDED-TRIANGLE envelope, NOT three separate circles.
- A bowl's top view is a CIRCLE if the bowl is perfectly round, but an ELLIPSE if (a) the bowl itself is oval-shaped, or (b) the viewing angle is not perfectly vertical.
- Knife top view = IRREGULAR shape: blade has a curved edge on one side and straight edge on the other, tapering to a point, plus a differently-shaped handle. It is NOT a simple rectangle.
- Glass jewelry box top view = RECTANGLE in most cases (length ≠ width), not a perfect square. Most jewelry boxes are elongated.
- Round lounge chair/daybed top view = OVAL/ELLIPTICAL ring (not a perfect circle), because the head-end and foot-end differ in width.
- Ancient vessel with lid, top view = CONCENTRIC CIRCLES: outer circle = vessel rim, inner circle = lid. If it has handles, they protrude OUTSIDE the outer circle.
- For SHADOW questions specifically: (1) Overhead noon sun → shadow ≈ top-view projection, (2) Angled sun → shadow is stretched/distorted, (3) Framework objects cast line shadows not solid shadows, (4) Transparent/translucent parts cast lighter or no shadow.
- Multiple clothespins stacked together: side profile shows CURVED outlines (each clothespin has curved arms), not straight lines.
- Hedge trimmer/pruning shears top view: complex irregular shape with handles + blade mechanism, hard to determine precisely → if uncertain, 'cannot determine' may be valid for unusual tools.

216.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- FUNDAMENTAL RULE: An object is stable if and only if the vertical projection of its center of gravity falls WITHIN its base of support (the contact area with the supporting surface).
- Wider base → more stable. Lower center of gravity → more stable. The most UNSTABLE object in a group is the one with the SMALLEST base of support relative to its height.
- A wooden spoon standing on its narrow handle edge is LESS stable than dried flowers in a bunch (flowers have a wider spread base). Narrow edge/line contact = extremely unstable.
- MASS RATIO RULE for perturbation: If perturbing_mass / structure_mass < 1:100, the perturbation has NEGLIGIBLE effect on normal structures. A bird (0.1-5kg) landing on a lamp post (50-200kg fixed to ground) causes NO noticeable effect.
- EXCEPTION to mass ratio rule: PRECISION-BALANCED structures (stone balancing, card houses, objects balanced on a point/edge) can be toppled by even 1:10000 mass ratios. A single water drop (~0.05g) CAN collapse a balanced stone structure (~5kg) by creating a tiny off-center moment.
- A water drop on a precision stone balance → structure WILL COLLAPSE. Categorize: is this a normal stable placement or a precision balance? Precision balances are inherently at the tipping point.
- HUMANS have ACTIVE balance recovery ability, unlike passive objects. An adult can recover from moderate pushes. A child has weaker recovery. On slippery/steep surfaces, recovery is harder. If center of gravity is already far outside the base (e.g., leaning far over a ladder edge), even adults may fail to recover.
- Decision tree for 'will person fall': (1) How far is COG from base edge? (2) Surface friction (wet/dry/steep)? (3) Can they grab something? (4) Adult or child? → Child on steep surface with no handhold and shifted COG = likely falls. Adult on flat surface with slight push = recovers.
- Removing an object from a stack: removing the TOP item makes the remaining stack MORE stable (lower COG). Removing the BOTTOM item causes upper items to DROP but often MAINTAIN their relative arrangement. Removing a MIDDLE item depends on whether upper items can bridge the gap.
- If each object in an arrangement has its OWN independent support on the table surface, removing one object does NOT affect others. Example: pencil resting on top of a walnut on a table → remove pencil → walnut stays (it has its own stable base on the table).
- Round objects on ANY tilted surface will roll. Flat-bottomed objects on slightly tilted surfaces usually stay put. A circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook → WILL ROLL OFF. A rectangular eraser on the same notebook → likely stays.
- Critical tilt angles: rough surface ~15° before sliding starts, smooth surface ~5-8°, round objects may roll at even 1-2° tilt.
- If an object appears to stand upright without visible support but physics says it shouldn't (e.g., a floppy bag standing upright), it is most likely LEANING AGAINST an unseen object outside the frame. Do not attribute it to the object's own shape.
- Pushing furniture: force applied ABOVE the center of gravity tends to cause TIPPING/TOPPLING. Force applied BELOW the center of gravity tends to cause SLIDING. Pushing the top of a chair backrest → the chair will likely TIP OVER, not slide.
- Sitting on the edge of a bench: if the person's weight is within the bench's base of support and the bench is heavy/stable, NOTHING happens. A light blanket draped over the bench does not slide off just because someone sits on one end (unless they directly push it).
- A child leaning outward on a ladder: the child's weight shifts the combined COG outside the ladder's base → the ladder WILL TIP. The child is a significant fraction of the ladder's weight (mass ratio 20kg child : 5kg ladder = 4:1 → very significant).
- Normal force explanation: a towel on a table doesn't fall through because the table exerts an upward NORMAL FORCE (Newton's 3rd law). However, if the question asks WHY the towel stays specifically at its position (not sliding off), the answer is that the towel's COG projection is within the table surface (it is in a STABLE EQUILIBRIUM position).

222.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In REAL environments (not vacuum), air resistance is significant for light, flat, or large-surface-area objects. A camera falls FASTER than a wide-brimmed hat. A coin falls FASTER than a sheet of paper.
- Fall speed ranking (fastest to slowest): solid metal/glass/stone → electronics/tools/ceramics → books/wood/fruit → hats/paper cups/empty bottles → paper/feathers/thin fabric/plastic bags.
- When asked 'which hits the ground first', DEFAULT to considering air resistance UNLESS the question explicitly states vacuum/no air. If two objects have similar density and shape, answer 'nearly simultaneous but depends on conditions'.
- Water/liquid poured from a container follows a PARABOLIC (curved) trajectory, not a straight line. Side view shows a downward-curving arc. The curve is more pronounced at low flow rates or long distances.
- An object WILL ROLL on a tilted surface if its contact surface is CIRCULAR/CURVED: balls on any slope, bottles lying sideways, circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook.
- An object will NOT roll if its contact surface is FLAT: books, boxes, phones, cups standing upright. These will SLIDE only if the tilt exceeds the friction angle (typically >15° on rough surfaces, >5° on smooth).
- A cylindrical candle holder LYING ON ITS SIDE on a table will roll if the table has even slight tilt. It will roll until it hits an obstacle or falls off the edge.
- Elastic bounce: a soft cushion thrown up and landing on a sofa WILL BOUNCE due to the sofa's elasticity → it may bounce off the sofa onto the floor.
- Inertia principle: quickly pulling out the BOTTOM plate from a stack → upper plates momentarily stay in place (inertia) → then drop vertically onto the table surface → they usually REMAIN STACKED (not topple), similar to the tablecloth trick.
- DENSITY AND BUOYANCY: Whole lemon floats (skin has air pockets, overall density ≈ 0.9-1.0). But LEMON SLICES SINK (cutting exposes dense flesh, loses air pocket buoyancy, flesh density > 1.0).
- Floating objects: most wood, whole citrus fruits (lemon, orange), empty plastic bottles, ice, wax/candles, cork.
- Sinking objects: lemon/orange SLICES, grapes, fresh eggs, stones, metal, glass, ceramics, coins.
- OUTDOOR WIND is a primary force for lightweight objects. Leaves on a deck move to the pool primarily by WIND, not gravity (decks are usually horizontal). Consider wind before gravity for any outdoor scene with light objects.
- Wind pushes liquid surfaces: wind from direction X → liquid surface tilts toward the opposite direction → may spill from the downwind side of the container.
- Ball rolling and hitting an obstacle → BOUNCES/DEFLECTS away from the obstacle, does not simply stop next to it. The bounce direction depends on the angle of impact.
- Domino/chain reaction: one falling object may strike adjacent objects → assess whether neighboring objects are within reach and whether the falling object has enough momentum.
- A phone standing upright on its edge will eventually fall over. The primary cause is that its equilibrium is UNSTABLE (very narrow base), and any tiny perturbation (vibration, air current) will topple it. The proximate cause of perturbation may be air movement, surface vibration, etc., not 'gravity' (gravity is always present; the question is what TRIGGERS the fall).
- On an inclined book surface: heavy objects with flat bottoms (like oranges sitting in a slight depression, or nuts with irregular shapes that wedge in place) may NOT move at small angles due to friction and surface irregularity. Don't assume everything slides/rolls on any tilt.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shape Projection) ---
- Projection = the OUTLINE/SILHOUETTE seen along a specific viewing direction. It LOSES one dimension entirely and keeps the maximum extent in the other two dimensions.
- Cylinder: top view = CIRCLE, side view = RECTANGLE. This applies to cups, cans, candles, cylindrical lamp shades, pillars.
- Sphere: ALL views = CIRCLE. But an ELLIPSOID produces a CIRCLE only along its axis of symmetry; other views produce ELLIPSES.
- Cone: top view = CIRCLE (with center dot for apex), side view = TRIANGLE.
- Shallow tray/plate: top view = CIRCLE or RECTANGLE (its face shape), side view = a very thin LINE (nearly invisible, NOT a circle).
- A tennis/badminton racket head is an ELLIPSE, not a perfect circle. Its shadow shows an ELLIPTICAL outline with internal grid/string pattern.
- ATTACHMENTS NEVER DISAPPEAR in projection. Handles, spouts, knobs, bells, crowns, antennas ALL appear as additional shapes protruding from the main silhouette.
- Classic alarm clock top view = main body (circle or rectangle) + TWO SMALL CIRCLES on top (bells) + thin line (hammer). Not just a simple circle.
- Teapot/kettle side view = body silhouette + S-CURVED spout + C-CURVED handle + lid knob on top. Not just an oval.
- HOLLOW/MESH/FRAMEWORK structures cast LINE-PATTERN shadows, not solid shadows. Woven chair → striped shadow. Racket strings → grid shadow. Fence → parallel line shadow. Bicycle wheel spokes → radial line shadow.
- Chair side profile = inverted 'r' shape: vertical line (backrest) → horizontal line (seat) → vertical line down (front leg), with rear leg going diagonally from seat back to floor.
- Person riding a bicycle, viewed from DIRECTLY ABOVE = T-shape (body + handlebars form the horizontal bar, bicycle frame forms the vertical bar).
- When multiple spheres/rounded shapes are FUSED together (like Mickey Mouse shape = 1 large + 2 small spheres), the top-view outline is a CONTINUOUS ROUNDED-TRIANGLE envelope, NOT three separate circles.
- A bowl's top view is a CIRCLE if the bowl is perfectly round, but an ELLIPSE if (a) the bowl itself is oval-shaped, or (b) the viewing angle is not perfectly vertical.
- Knife top view = IRREGULAR shape: blade has a curved edge on one side and straight edge on the other, tapering to a point, plus a differently-shaped handle. It is NOT a simple rectangle.
- Glass jewelry box top view = RECTANGLE in most cases (length ≠ width), not a perfect square. Most jewelry boxes are elongated.
- Round lounge chair/daybed top view = OVAL/ELLIPTICAL ring (not a perfect circle), because the head-end and foot-end differ in width.
- Ancient vessel with lid, top view = CONCENTRIC CIRCLES: outer circle = vessel rim, inner circle = lid. If it has handles, they protrude OUTSIDE the outer circle.
- For SHADOW questions specifically: (1) Overhead noon sun → shadow ≈ top-view projection, (2) Angled sun → shadow is stretched/distorted, (3) Framework objects cast line shadows not solid shadows, (4) Transparent/translucent parts cast lighter or no shadow.
- Multiple clothespins stacked together: side profile shows CURVED outlines (each clothespin has curved arms), not straight lines.
- Hedge trimmer/pruning shears top view: complex irregular shape with handles + blade mechanism, hard to determine precisely → if uncertain, 'cannot determine' may be valid for unusual tools.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Spatial Containment) ---
- A container MUST be hollow or have an opening large enough for the object to pass through.
- Always check the OPENING DIAMETER of the container against the MAXIMUM CROSS-SECTION of the object, not just the main body.
- Protruding parts (handles, spouts, knobs, antennas, decorations) INCREASE the effective bounding box of an object. A teapot with handle can be 50-70% wider than its body alone.
- If the object's height EXCEEDS the container's internal depth, it can only be PARTIALLY inserted (sticks out above the rim), not fully contained.
- Check whether the container already holds other items inside. Existing items REDUCE available internal volume and may need to be removed first.
- Rigid objects (metal, glass, ceramic) must strictly satisfy dimensional constraints. Flexible objects (cloth, paper, rope) can bend or fold to fit smaller spaces.
- Consider BOTH orientations: an object that is too long horizontally may fit vertically, and vice versa. Always check the best-fit orientation.
- For building/doorway passage: width AND height AND ground-level obstacles (steps, curbs, thresholds) must ALL be checked. A vehicle may fit width-wise but be blocked by stairs.
- Public art installations of everyday objects (giant dustpan, giant chair) are 10-100x larger than real counterparts. Use nearby humans (~170cm tall) as size reference.
- Common container internal dimensions: standard mug opening ~8-9cm, cereal bowl ~14-16cm, shoebox ~30×18×11cm, standard doorway ~80-90cm wide × 200-210cm tall.
- Common object maximum cross-sections: apple ~7-9cm, baby head width ~11-15cm (newborn 11cm, 6-month 13-15cm), teapot with handle ~20cm, bicycle handlebar ~45-65cm.
- When the answer involves 'fits but tightly' vs 'fits with plenty of room': if the size margin is <20% of the container dimension, it is TIGHT; if >20%, there is ROOM TO SPARE.
- A bowl inverted over a cup as a lid: compare the BOWL'S RIM DIAMETER with the CUP'S RIM DIAMETER. If bowl rim < cup rim, the bowl falls inside rather than sitting on top.
- For 'can X pass through Y' questions: the object's MINIMUM cross-section along the direction of travel determines passability, not its volume.

310.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- FUNDAMENTAL RULE: An object is stable if and only if the vertical projection of its center of gravity falls WITHIN its base of support (the contact area with the supporting surface).
- Wider base → more stable. Lower center of gravity → more stable. The most UNSTABLE object in a group is the one with the SMALLEST base of support relative to its height.
- A wooden spoon standing on its narrow handle edge is LESS stable than dried flowers in a bunch (flowers have a wider spread base). Narrow edge/line contact = extremely unstable.
- MASS RATIO RULE for perturbation: If perturbing_mass / structure_mass < 1:100, the perturbation has NEGLIGIBLE effect on normal structures. A bird (0.1-5kg) landing on a lamp post (50-200kg fixed to ground) causes NO noticeable effect.
- EXCEPTION to mass ratio rule: PRECISION-BALANCED structures (stone balancing, card houses, objects balanced on a point/edge) can be toppled by even 1:10000 mass ratios. A single water drop (~0.05g) CAN collapse a balanced stone structure (~5kg) by creating a tiny off-center moment.
- A water drop on a precision stone balance → structure WILL COLLAPSE. Categorize: is this a normal stable placement or a precision balance? Precision balances are inherently at the tipping point.
- HUMANS have ACTIVE balance recovery ability, unlike passive objects. An adult can recover from moderate pushes. A child has weaker recovery. On slippery/steep surfaces, recovery is harder. If center of gravity is already far outside the base (e.g., leaning far over a ladder edge), even adults may fail to recover.
- Decision tree for 'will person fall': (1) How far is COG from base edge? (2) Surface friction (wet/dry/steep)? (3) Can they grab something? (4) Adult or child? → Child on steep surface with no handhold and shifted COG = likely falls. Adult on flat surface with slight push = recovers.
- Removing an object from a stack: removing the TOP item makes the remaining stack MORE stable (lower COG). Removing the BOTTOM item causes upper items to DROP but often MAINTAIN their relative arrangement. Removing a MIDDLE item depends on whether upper items can bridge the gap.
- If each object in an arrangement has its OWN independent support on the table surface, removing one object does NOT affect others. Example: pencil resting on top of a walnut on a table → remove pencil → walnut stays (it has its own stable base on the table).
- Round objects on ANY tilted surface will roll. Flat-bottomed objects on slightly tilted surfaces usually stay put. A circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook → WILL ROLL OFF. A rectangular eraser on the same notebook → likely stays.
- Critical tilt angles: rough surface ~15° before sliding starts, smooth surface ~5-8°, round objects may roll at even 1-2° tilt.
- If an object appears to stand upright without visible support but physics says it shouldn't (e.g., a floppy bag standing upright), it is most likely LEANING AGAINST an unseen object outside the frame. Do not attribute it to the object's own shape.
- Pushing furniture: force applied ABOVE the center of gravity tends to cause TIPPING/TOPPLING. Force applied BELOW the center of gravity tends to cause SLIDING. Pushing the top of a chair backrest → the chair will likely TIP OVER, not slide.
- Sitting on the edge of a bench: if the person's weight is within the bench's base of support and the bench is heavy/stable, NOTHING happens. A light blanket draped over the bench does not slide off just because someone sits on one end (unless they directly push it).
- A child leaning outward on a ladder: the child's weight shifts the combined COG outside the ladder's base → the ladder WILL TIP. The child is a significant fraction of the ladder's weight (mass ratio 20kg child : 5kg ladder = 4:1 → very significant).
- Normal force explanation: a towel on a table doesn't fall through because the table exerts an upward NORMAL FORCE (Newton's 3rd law). However, if the question asks WHY the towel stays specifically at its position (not sliding off), the answer is that the towel's COG projection is within the table surface (it is in a STABLE EQUILIBRIUM position).

- [clock tower]: Presents a 'frontal' view. It is aligned with the camera axis.

- [Horizontal]: clock tower is to the LEFT of church.

=== Pairwise Geometric & Orientation Deductions ===

- [Relative Direction]: Both point 'up'. They are facing the SAME direction.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shape Projection) ---
- Projection = the OUTLINE/SILHOUETTE seen along a specific viewing direction. It LOSES one dimension entirely and keeps the maximum extent in the other two dimensions.
- Cylinder: top view = CIRCLE, side view = RECTANGLE. This applies to cups, cans, candles, cylindrical lamp shades, pillars.
- Sphere: ALL views = CIRCLE. But an ELLIPSOID produces a CIRCLE only along its axis of symmetry; other views produce ELLIPSES.
- Cone: top view = CIRCLE (with center dot for apex), side view = TRIANGLE.
- Shallow tray/plate: top view = CIRCLE or RECTANGLE (its face shape), side view = a very thin LINE (nearly invisible, NOT a circle).
- A tennis/badminton racket head is an ELLIPSE, not a perfect circle. Its shadow shows an ELLIPTICAL outline with internal grid/string pattern.
- ATTACHMENTS NEVER DISAPPEAR in projection. Handles, spouts, knobs, bells, crowns, antennas ALL appear as additional shapes protruding from the main silhouette.
- Classic alarm clock top view = main body (circle or rectangle) + TWO SMALL CIRCLES on top (bells) + thin line (hammer). Not just a simple circle.
- Teapot/kettle side view = body silhouette + S-CURVED spout + C-CURVED handle + lid knob on top. Not just an oval.
- HOLLOW/MESH/FRAMEWORK structures cast LINE-PATTERN shadows, not solid shadows. Woven chair → striped shadow. Racket strings → grid shadow. Fence → parallel line shadow. Bicycle wheel spokes → radial line shadow.
- Chair side profile = inverted 'r' shape: vertical line (backrest) → horizontal line (seat) → vertical line down (front leg), with rear leg going diagonally from seat back to floor.
- Person riding a bicycle, viewed from DIRECTLY ABOVE = T-shape (body + handlebars form the horizontal bar, bicycle frame forms the vertical bar).
- When multiple spheres/rounded shapes are FUSED together (like Mickey Mouse shape = 1 large + 2 small spheres), the top-view outline is a CONTINUOUS ROUNDED-TRIANGLE envelope, NOT three separate circles.
- A bowl's top view is a CIRCLE if the bowl is perfectly round, but an ELLIPSE if (a) the bowl itself is oval-shaped, or (b) the viewing angle is not perfectly vertical.
- Knife top view = IRREGULAR shape: blade has a curved edge on one side and straight edge on the other, tapering to a point, plus a differently-shaped handle. It is NOT a simple rectangle.
- Glass jewelry box top view = RECTANGLE in most cases (length ≠ width), not a perfect square. Most jewelry boxes are elongated.
- Round lounge chair/daybed top view = OVAL/ELLIPTICAL ring (not a perfect circle), because the head-end and foot-end differ in width.
- Ancient vessel with lid, top view = CONCENTRIC CIRCLES: outer circle = vessel rim, inner circle = lid. If it has handles, they protrude OUTSIDE the outer circle.
- For SHADOW questions specifically: (1) Overhead noon sun → shadow ≈ top-view projection, (2) Angled sun → shadow is stretched/distorted, (3) Framework objects cast line shadows not solid shadows, (4) Transparent/translucent parts cast lighter or no shadow.
- Multiple clothespins stacked together: side profile shows CURVED outlines (each clothespin has curved arms), not straight lines.
- Hedge trimmer/pruning shears top view: complex irregular shape with handles + blade mechanism, hard to determine precisely → if uncertain, 'cannot determine' may be valid for unusual tools.

356.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- In REAL environments (not vacuum), air resistance is significant for light, flat, or large-surface-area objects. A camera falls FASTER than a wide-brimmed hat. A coin falls FASTER than a sheet of paper.
- Fall speed ranking (fastest to slowest): solid metal/glass/stone → electronics/tools/ceramics → books/wood/fruit → hats/paper cups/empty bottles → paper/feathers/thin fabric/plastic bags.
- When asked 'which hits the ground first', DEFAULT to considering air resistance UNLESS the question explicitly states vacuum/no air. If two objects have similar density and shape, answer 'nearly simultaneous but depends on conditions'.
- Water/liquid poured from a container follows a PARABOLIC (curved) trajectory, not a straight line. Side view shows a downward-curving arc. The curve is more pronounced at low flow rates or long distances.
- An object WILL ROLL on a tilted surface if its contact surface is CIRCULAR/CURVED: balls on any slope, bottles lying sideways, circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook.
- An object will NOT roll if its contact surface is FLAT: books, boxes, phones, cups standing upright. These will SLIDE only if the tilt exceeds the friction angle (typically >15° on rough surfaces, >5° on smooth).
- A cylindrical candle holder LYING ON ITS SIDE on a table will roll if the table has even slight tilt. It will roll until it hits an obstacle or falls off the edge.
- Elastic bounce: a soft cushion thrown up and landing on a sofa WILL BOUNCE due to the sofa's elasticity → it may bounce off the sofa onto the floor.
- Inertia principle: quickly pulling out the BOTTOM plate from a stack → upper plates momentarily stay in place (inertia) → then drop vertically onto the table surface → they usually REMAIN STACKED (not topple), similar to the tablecloth trick.
- DENSITY AND BUOYANCY: Whole lemon floats (skin has air pockets, overall density ≈ 0.9-1.0). But LEMON SLICES SINK (cutting exposes dense flesh, loses air pocket buoyancy, flesh density > 1.0).
- Floating objects: most wood, whole citrus fruits (lemon, orange), empty plastic bottles, ice, wax/candles, cork.
- Sinking objects: lemon/orange SLICES, grapes, fresh eggs, stones, metal, glass, ceramics, coins.
- OUTDOOR WIND is a primary force for lightweight objects. Leaves on a deck move to the pool primarily by WIND, not gravity (decks are usually horizontal). Consider wind before gravity for any outdoor scene with light objects.
- Wind pushes liquid surfaces: wind from direction X → liquid surface tilts toward the opposite direction → may spill from the downwind side of the container.
- Ball rolling and hitting an obstacle → BOUNCES/DEFLECTS away from the obstacle, does not simply stop next to it. The bounce direction depends on the angle of impact.
- Domino/chain reaction: one falling object may strike adjacent objects → assess whether neighboring objects are within reach and whether the falling object has enough momentum.
- A phone standing upright on its edge will eventually fall over. The primary cause is that its equilibrium is UNSTABLE (very narrow base), and any tiny perturbation (vibration, air current) will topple it. The proximate cause of perturbation may be air movement, surface vibration, etc., not 'gravity' (gravity is always present; the question is what TRIGGERS the fall).
- On an inclined book surface: heavy objects with flat bottoms (like oranges sitting in a slight depression, or nuts with irregular shapes that wedge in place) may NOT move at small angles due to friction and surface irregularity. Don't assume everything slides/rolls on any tilt.

376.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- FUNDAMENTAL RULE: An object is stable if and only if the vertical projection of its center of gravity falls WITHIN its base of support (the contact area with the supporting surface).
- Wider base → more stable. Lower center of gravity → more stable. The most UNSTABLE object in a group is the one with the SMALLEST base of support relative to its height.
- A wooden spoon standing on its narrow handle edge is LESS stable than dried flowers in a bunch (flowers have a wider spread base). Narrow edge/line contact = extremely unstable.
- MASS RATIO RULE for perturbation: If perturbing_mass / structure_mass < 1:100, the perturbation has NEGLIGIBLE effect on normal structures. A bird (0.1-5kg) landing on a lamp post (50-200kg fixed to ground) causes NO noticeable effect.
- EXCEPTION to mass ratio rule: PRECISION-BALANCED structures (stone balancing, card houses, objects balanced on a point/edge) can be toppled by even 1:10000 mass ratios. A single water drop (~0.05g) CAN collapse a balanced stone structure (~5kg) by creating a tiny off-center moment.
- A water drop on a precision stone balance → structure WILL COLLAPSE. Categorize: is this a normal stable placement or a precision balance? Precision balances are inherently at the tipping point.
- HUMANS have ACTIVE balance recovery ability, unlike passive objects. An adult can recover from moderate pushes. A child has weaker recovery. On slippery/steep surfaces, recovery is harder. If center of gravity is already far outside the base (e.g., leaning far over a ladder edge), even adults may fail to recover.
- Decision tree for 'will person fall': (1) How far is COG from base edge? (2) Surface friction (wet/dry/steep)? (3) Can they grab something? (4) Adult or child? → Child on steep surface with no handhold and shifted COG = likely falls. Adult on flat surface with slight push = recovers.
- Removing an object from a stack: removing the TOP item makes the remaining stack MORE stable (lower COG). Removing the BOTTOM item causes upper items to DROP but often MAINTAIN their relative arrangement. Removing a MIDDLE item depends on whether upper items can bridge the gap.
- If each object in an arrangement has its OWN independent support on the table surface, removing one object does NOT affect others. Example: pencil resting on top of a walnut on a table → remove pencil → walnut stays (it has its own stable base on the table).
- Round objects on ANY tilted surface will roll. Flat-bottomed objects on slightly tilted surfaces usually stay put. A circular magnifying glass frame on a tilted notebook → WILL ROLL OFF. A rectangular eraser on the same notebook → likely stays.
- Critical tilt angles: rough surface ~15° before sliding starts, smooth surface ~5-8°, round objects may roll at even 1-2° tilt.
- If an object appears to stand upright without visible support but physics says it shouldn't (e.g., a floppy bag standing upright), it is most likely LEANING AGAINST an unseen object outside the frame. Do not attribute it to the object's own shape.
- Pushing furniture: force applied ABOVE the center of gravity tends to cause TIPPING/TOPPLING. Force applied BELOW the center of gravity tends to cause SLIDING. Pushing the top of a chair backrest → the chair will likely TIP OVER, not slide.
- Sitting on the edge of a bench: if the person's weight is within the bench's base of support and the bench is heavy/stable, NOTHING happens. A light blanket draped over the bench does not slide off just because someone sits on one end (unless they directly push it).
- A child leaning outward on a ladder: the child's weight shifts the combined COG outside the ladder's base → the ladder WILL TIP. The child is a significant fraction of the ladder's weight (mass ratio 20kg child : 5kg ladder = 4:1 → very significant).
- Normal force explanation: a towel on a table doesn't fall through because the table exerts an upward NORMAL FORCE (Newton's 3rd law). However, if the question asks WHY the towel stays specifically at its position (not sliding off), the answer is that the towel's COG projection is within the table surface (it is in a STABLE EQUILIBRIUM position).

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Shape Projection) ---
- Projection = the OUTLINE/SILHOUETTE seen along a specific viewing direction. It LOSES one dimension entirely and keeps the maximum extent in the other two dimensions.
- Cylinder: top view = CIRCLE, side view = RECTANGLE. This applies to cups, cans, candles, cylindrical lamp shades, pillars.
- Sphere: ALL views = CIRCLE. But an ELLIPSOID produces a CIRCLE only along its axis of symmetry; other views produce ELLIPSES.
- Cone: top view = CIRCLE (with center dot for apex), side view = TRIANGLE.
- Shallow tray/plate: top view = CIRCLE or RECTANGLE (its face shape), side view = a very thin LINE (nearly invisible, NOT a circle).
- A tennis/badminton racket head is an ELLIPSE, not a perfect circle. Its shadow shows an ELLIPTICAL outline with internal grid/string pattern.
- ATTACHMENTS NEVER DISAPPEAR in projection. Handles, spouts, knobs, bells, crowns, antennas ALL appear as additional shapes protruding from the main silhouette.
- Classic alarm clock top view = main body (circle or rectangle) + TWO SMALL CIRCLES on top (bells) + thin line (hammer). Not just a simple circle.
- Teapot/kettle side view = body silhouette + S-CURVED spout + C-CURVED handle + lid knob on top. Not just an oval.
- HOLLOW/MESH/FRAMEWORK structures cast LINE-PATTERN shadows, not solid shadows. Woven chair → striped shadow. Racket strings → grid shadow. Fence → parallel line shadow. Bicycle wheel spokes → radial line shadow.
- Chair side profile = inverted 'r' shape: vertical line (backrest) → horizontal line (seat) → vertical line down (front leg), with rear leg going diagonally from seat back to floor.
- Person riding a bicycle, viewed from DIRECTLY ABOVE = T-shape (body + handlebars form the horizontal bar, bicycle frame forms the vertical bar).
- When multiple spheres/rounded shapes are FUSED together (like Mickey Mouse shape = 1 large + 2 small spheres), the top-view outline is a CONTINUOUS ROUNDED-TRIANGLE envelope, NOT three separate circles.
- A bowl's top view is a CIRCLE if the bowl is perfectly round, but an ELLIPSE if (a) the bowl itself is oval-shaped, or (b) the viewing angle is not perfectly vertical.
- Knife top view = IRREGULAR shape: blade has a curved edge on one side and straight edge on the other, tapering to a point, plus a differently-shaped handle. It is NOT a simple rectangle.
- Glass jewelry box top view = RECTANGLE in most cases (length ≠ width), not a perfect square. Most jewelry boxes are elongated.
- Round lounge chair/daybed top view = OVAL/ELLIPTICAL ring (not a perfect circle), because the head-end and foot-end differ in width.
- Ancient vessel with lid, top view = CONCENTRIC CIRCLES: outer circle = vessel rim, inner circle = lid. If it has handles, they protrude OUTSIDE the outer circle.
- For SHADOW questions specifically: (1) Overhead noon sun → shadow ≈ top-view projection, (2) Angled sun → shadow is stretched/distorted, (3) Framework objects cast line shadows not solid shadows, (4) Transparent/translucent parts cast lighter or no shadow.
- Multiple clothespins stacked together: side profile shows CURVED outlines (each clothespin has curved arms), not straight lines.
- Hedge trimmer/pruning shears top view: complex irregular shape with handles + blade mechanism, hard to determine precisely → if uncertain, 'cannot determine' may be valid for unusual tools.
